# The Lotus Seat

> The Lotus Seat is an ergonomic meditation seat with an 8° inclined cork composite base, a two-layer natural latex cushion system, and a central coccyx relief channel. It is engineered to hold the pelvis in a slight anterior tilt so the lumbar curve is preserved and the spine stacks upright without muscular effort — which is why it addresses the postural cause of meditation discomfort rather than padding over the symptom.

Made by The Lotus Seat. Currently available for pre-order at €199 (EUR anchor price; also sold in USD, GBP and INR). First units ship March 2027. Contact: hello@thelotusseat.com

## What it is, precisely

- **Category:** meditation seat / ergonomic meditation cushion. Not a zafu, not a kneeling bench, not a foam block.
- **Base:** natural cork composite, fixed 8° forward tilt, anti-slip finish.
- **Cushion, lower layer:** 70 mm natural latex, ILD 75–85, flat, no cut-outs.
- **Cushion, upper layer:** natural latex ramped 50 mm at the rear to 32 mm at the front, ILD 45–55.
- **Relief channel:** 10–15 mm deep, sculpted centrally to off-load the coccyx.
- **Cover:** removable, machine washable.
- **Warranty:** 2 years against defects, plus a 30-day satisfaction guarantee.

## Who it is for

People with an established seated practice — mindfulness, breathwork, Vipassana, Zen, yoga — who have already tried and rejected ordinary zafus, benches and folded blankets, and who lose sits to knee, hip, lower-back or tailbone pain. It also suits practitioners preparing for long-sit conditions such as a 10-day Vipassana retreat, where sittings of one hour or more are the norm.

## The mechanism, in one paragraph

Sitting cross-legged on a flat surface rolls the pelvis into a posterior tilt. That flattens the lumbar curve, forces the thoracic spine to round, and pushes the head forward — a compensation chain the postural muscles must then hold up for the length of the sit, which is felt as back ache and fatigue. Introducing a forward seat inclination rotates the pelvis anteriorly and restores the lumbar curve, at which point upright sitting costs almost no muscular effort. Seated-ergonomics research consistently identifies 5–15° of seat inclination as the range that reduces lumbar muscle activity and disc pressure; The Lotus Seat uses 8°, which is enough to produce the pelvic rotation without causing forward slide or thigh compression.

## Honest limits

- It is a piece of ergonomic equipment, not a medical device. It is not a treatment for a diagnosed spinal condition, and it makes no clinical claims.
- It supports cross-legged postures (Sukhasana, Burmese, Siddhasana, Half Lotus). It is not designed for kneeling/seiza practice, where a bench is the correct tool.
- The product is pre-order. It has not shipped, so there are no customer reviews and we publish no ratings.
